claim: Independent verification confirms YOLO provider dispatch API implements provider-specific state isolation and tool boundary enforcement in demand evidence mode, with one critical finding: provider normalization in executionConfig() may not cover all provider variants, creating a potential state leakage path.

evidence.summary: Confirmed CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_TOOLS array exists with 8 tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, WebFetch, WebSearch, Agent). DEMAND_EVIDENCE_CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_SETTINGS sets permission_mode to 'auto' and disables session persistence.
evidence.why: Independent read confirms the tool boundary definition matches explorer claim.

evidence.summary: ALLOWED_WRITE_TOOLS includes Write and Edit tools. evidenceAgentWriteRoot returns a timestamped path under .yolo/demand/evidence/dispatch.
evidence.why: Confirms the allowed write root mechanism for evidence agents.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot() uses git ls-files to get project files, computes sha256 digest for each, and stores in a Map. diffBoundarySnapshots() compares digests to detect added/modified/deleted files outside allowed_write_roots.
evidence.why: Independent verification confirms boundary snapshot implementation matches explorer description.

evidence.summary: CRITICAL FINDING: executionConfig() has explicit branches for 'claude' (lines 352-390), 'codex' (lines 391-393), and 'custom' (lines 394-398). However, there is no else/default branch. If a non-normalized provider string reaches this function, it falls through without setting provider-specific configs, potentially causing state leakage.
evidence.why: This is a gap in provider isolation that explorer did not identify as a risk.

evidence.summary: Claude branch sets claude_tools='default', claude_allowed_tools=[all tools], claude_disallowed_tools=[], claude_permission_mode='auto', claude_persist_session=false, and applies DEMAND_EVIDENCE_CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_SETTINGS.
evidence.why: Confirms Claude provider gets full tool capability under boundary settings.

evidence.summary: Codex branch sets config.ai.provider='codex', codex_sandbox='boundary', codex_approval='auto', and codex_model='gpt-4o' if not set.
evidence.why: Confirms Codex provider isolation through sandbox boundary and explicit model.

evidence.summary: Custom branch sets config.ai.provider='custom', custom_sandbox='boundary', and preserves custom_command if already set.
evidence.why: Confirms custom provider gets sandbox boundary.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation() switches on provider: codex uses --sandbox/--cd/--output-last-message flags, claude uses --permission-mode/--settings/--tools flags, custom uses shell -c with command. No default case exists.
evidence.why: Independent verification confirms provider-specific CLI invocation logic.

evidence.summary: PROVIDER_ALIASES map has 9 entries normalizing variants: anthropic/claude→claude, codex/openai→codex, openai→codex, gpt→codex, custom/shell/local→custom. normalizeAgentProvider() uses this map with fallback to 'claude'.
evidence.why: Confirms provider normalization prevents most alias ambiguity, with claude as safe default.

evidence.summary: buildAgentAdapterCapabilities() returns object with provider-specific boolean flags: codex has sandbox/approval_policy/file_write/shell_exec, claude has no sandbox but permission_mode, custom has custom_sandbox and custom_command.
evidence.why: Confirms provider capability contracts are declaratively separated.

evidence.summary: Test file imports the actual evidence-dispatch module, not mocks. Tests use describe/it from a test framework (likely mocha/jest).
evidence.why: Confirms tests execute real implementation, not mocked behavior.

evidence.summary: Test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' calls buildDemandEvidenceDispatch() with {provider: 'codex'}, then asserts config.ai.provider==='codex', !config.ai.codex_sandbox, and config.ai.codex_approval==='auto'.
evidence.why: Confirms executable test validates provider isolation.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude dispatch keeps tools available and relies on boundary enforcement' asserts claude_tools==='default', claude_allowed_tools.includes('WebFetch'), claude_allowed_tools.includes('Write'), and settings.permissions.allow.includes('Read').
evidence.why: Confirms test validates Claude tool capability under boundary.

evidence.summary: Test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' writes test-project.txt after buildBoundarySnapshot(), then asserts status==='blocked', boundary.project_mutation==='violated', and blockers[0].code==='BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION'.
evidence.why: Confirms boundary audit detects and blocks project mutations.

evidence.summary: Line 443 calls const before = buildBoundarySnapshot(config, allowed_write_roots) before agent execution.
evidence.why: Confirms baseline snapshot is captured pre-execution as explorer claimed.

evidence.summary: Lines 486-492 call const boundary = diffBoundarySnapshots(before, after, allowed_write_roots) and if boundary.project_mutation === 'violated' create BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker.
evidence.why: Confirms post-execution boundary diff creates blockers for violations.

assumptions: normalizeAgentProvider() is always called before executionConfig(), ensuring no unnormalized provider strings reach the branching logic.
Git workspace is clean during snapshot (no uncommitted changes that could skew digest comparison).
sha256 digests are sufficient for detecting mutation (file content changes, not permission/metadata changes).
risks: CRITICAL: executionConfig() and buildProviderInvocation() have no default/else branch. If a new provider variant is added to PROVIDER_ALIASES but executionConfig() is not updated, the function will fall through without setting provider-specific configs, causing state leakage.
If normalizeAgentProvider() is bypassed or called with a non-standard provider string, executionConfig() will not apply isolation settings.
Boundary snapshot only captures files tracked by git. Untracked files that are modified would not be detected.
Concurrent file modifications during snapshot could create race conditions (though unlikely in single-threaded execution).
missing: Test coverage for executionConfig() fall-through scenario (what happens when provider doesn't match any branch?).
Test coverage for untracked file mutations (boundary snapshot only covers git-tracked files).
Integration test with real Codex provider execution (not just config validation).
Test for provider alias edge cases (empty string, null, undefined provider).

result.notes: Independent verification largely confirms explorer's claim. Provider dispatch API implements state isolation through: (1) PROVIDER_ALIASES + normalizeAgentProvider() prevent ambiguity with claude fallback, (2) buildAgentAdapterCapabilities() declaratively separates provider capabilities, (3) executionConfig() branches apply provider-specific settings (Claude boundary tools, no session; Codex sandbox boundary; Custom sandbox boundary), (4) buildProviderInvocation() generates provider-specific CLI args, (5) boundary snapshot/diff audits detect project mutations, (6) tests verify isolation and blocking. CRITICAL GAP FOUND: executionConfig() lacks default/else branch, creating fall-through risk if provider normalization fails or new provider is added without updating all three locations (PROVIDER_ALIASES, executionConfig, buildProviderInvocation). This is a real state leakage path that explorer did not flag. Recommendation: Add explicit else branch that throws error or applies safe defaults (claude mode with read-only boundary).
